title: The Life-Phase Thread
thesis: The attention economy does not affect Indians uniformly; it imposes distinct harms at each life stage, and those harms compound across generations.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: The attention economy does not affect Indians uniformly; it imposes distinct harms at each life stage, and those harms compound across generations.

evidence.snippet: ASER 2024 finds that 76% of 14–16-year-olds use smartphones for social media while 57% use them for education, and only 8% of parents see children as addiction-free.
Gallup places India's employee engagement at 23% and estimates $351 billion in annual disengagement losses.
A LASI-based comparative study finds 91% of older Indian adults are digitally excluded, the highest among lower-middle-income countries studied.

counterevidence.summary: Life-stage effects are shaped by income, education, family structure, and regional variation, not only by platform design.

claims.claim: Heavy recreational screen use begins in childhood, with nearly half of urban parents reporting children 9–17 spend three or more hours daily on social media, video, and games.

evidence.snippet: LocalCircles 2025 survey of 57,000+ urban parents: 49% of children aged 9–17 spend 3+ hours/day on social media, video/OTT, and online games; 22% spend more than 6 hours/day.
ASER 2024 reports only 8% of parents of 14–16-year-olds see their children as addiction-free.

counterevidence.summary: LocalCircles is a self-reported, urban, crowd-sourced survey and may over-represent concerned parents; ASER covers rural districts and different age framing.

claims.claim: Indian students face a split-screen adolescence in which recreational smartphone use outpaces educational use and academic anxiety is reported by a large majority.

evidence.snippet: Among 14–16-year-olds who can use a smartphone, 76% used it for social media while 57% used it for education in the reference week.
NCERT 2022 student mental-health survey (n=379,013) found 81% of students anxious about studies, exams, and results, and 43% reported mood swings.

counterevidence.summary: The same device is used for both social and educational purposes; anxiety has many sources beyond screen time, and the survey measures self-reported feelings, not clinical diagnoses.

claims.claim: India's young workers enter labour markets where engagement is low, attention is fragmented, and digital distraction imposes a large economic cost.

evidence.snippet: Gallup State of the Global Workplace 2025 India data: 23% employee engagement, $351 billion annual disengagement cost.

counterevidence.summary: Engagement and productivity are also driven by wages, job quality, management, and macroeconomic conditions, not only digital distraction.

claims.claim: Heavy smartphone use is associated with harm to marital relationships and a weakening of community participation, suggesting the attention economy extracts from social fabric as well as individual time.

evidence.snippet: vivo Switch Off Study 2022 (n=1,000+ married couples): 88% agreed excessive smartphone use is hurting their relationship with their spouse.
NSSO Time Use Survey 2024 reports volunteer/community participation down more than 50% since 2019.

counterevidence.summary: The vivo study is industry-funded; relationship harm and community participation decline have multiple causes, including the pandemic and urbanisation.

claims.claim: Older Indians are largely left out of the digital infrastructure, with roughly nine in ten older adults digitally excluded.

evidence.snippet: LASI-based comparative analysis (MDPI 2024) finds 91% of older adults in India digitally excluded, defined by lack of internet and social-media use over the past month.

counterevidence.summary: The study defines digital exclusion by recent internet and social-media use; some older adults may be supported by family members who transact on their behalf.

claims.claim: The harms of attention extraction are connected across generations, so solutions that only fix one life stage are likely to leak back into the others.

evidence.snippet: ASER 2024 shows adolescent entertainment-first phone use, suggesting childhood defaults shape later educational and work behaviour.
High elderly digital exclusion sits at the far end of the same digital transition that captures youth attention.

counterevidence.summary: Generational links are plausible but not proven by the cited studies; other social and economic factors also drive outcomes at each life stage.

sources.title: Ministry of Education: NCERT Student Mental Health Survey 2022 (Parliamentary Release)
LocalCircles: Children, Social Media and Online Gaming Addiction Survey 2025
Gallup State of the Global Workplace: India Country-Level Data
vivo Switch Off Study on Impact of Smartphones on Spousal Relationships
MDPI Sustainability: Ensuring Sustainable Digital Inclusion among the Elderly (LASI-based analysis)

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and keep each life-stage claim separate from the others.
Preserve the plain-language, evidence-cautious tone; do not shame individuals for screen use.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; flag industry-funded or self-reported data.
Distinguish India-specific measurements from global benchmarks, especially for workplace interruption and focus-session data.
Link back to related series articles only when the user asks for the broader arc.
